text: The surgeon begins the nephrectomy procedure by mobilizing the colon and dissecting the mesocolon away from Drota's fascia, using robotic instruments for precise manipulation.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and clips the mid-ureter without dividing it, ensuring tumor cells do not migrate into the bladder during manipulation of the kidney.

The surgeon dissects the ureter down to the bladder, using the fourth arm instrument to apply traction and improve exposure in the pelvis, facilitating the dissection process while the ureter remains under tension.
The surgeon dissects the ureter into the pelvis, revealing the detrusor fibers as the ureter widens upon entering the bladder.

The surgeon enters the bladder mucosa and anchors a barbed suture to the bladder opening. The bladder cuff is sequentially released using scissors and then sutured to prevent retraction of the bladder opening. Care is taken to avoid complete transection of the bladder cuff.

The surgeon identifies the ureteral orifice and completely releases the bladder cuff. Following this, the cystotomy is closed in two layers using a barbed suture. The bladder is filled with fluid to confirm a watertight closure.
The surgeon focuses on the kidney to complete the nephrectomy, isolating the renal vein and renal artery. The renal artery and vein are divided using an endoscopic stapler.

The surgeon develops the plane between the upper pole of the kidney and the adrenal gland, ensuring the adrenal gland remains in situ. The posterior and lateral attachments of the kidney are divided, releasing the kidney from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon performs a retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, isolating the periaortic lymphatics starting at the renal hilum and moving inferiorly. Polymer clips are used to control the lymphatic tissue surrounding the aorta, and the inferior extent of the lymphatic packet is secured with additional clips. The lymphatic packet is then placed into an endoscopic retrieval bag.
